video_title: Has Trend Following Changed Forever? | Systematic Investor | Ep.408
gist: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen and Alan Dunne review a dispersed first half of 2026 for CTAs, unpack how new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's pullback from forward guidance could add volatility and favor trend-following, then dig into three new papers: two arguing trend following is a structural portfolio diversifier for a post-globalization regime, and a CFM paper (with Jean-Philippe Bouchaud) contending that fast trend following's decline since 2010 is driven by volatility-adjusted tick size and the breakdown of the bank-warehousing execution mechanism, not AUM growth or electronification.

themes.theme: Fed regime change under Kevin Warsh

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's first press conference as Fed chair was notably evasive on his economic views, featured a shorter post-meeting statement than under his predecessor, and he declined to submit a dot to the Fed's dot plot, signaling a deliberate retreat from detailed forward guidance.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Watch for reduced Fed transparency ahead, which could raise policy-driven volatility and shift markets toward relying on data over Fed rhetoric.

insights.claim: Warsh has commissioned an internal Fed task force on communications and is skeptical of forward guidance, arguing markets have become overly reliant on it and that the Fed should let markets draw their own inferences from economic data rather than try to anticipate the Fed's next move.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: A shift away from scripted Fed communication changes how bond markets should price the future rate path.

insights.claim: A study found that 68% of the variance in the 5-year Treasury yield around a Fed meeting was attributable to the Fed's statement, versus only 12% to the actual rate decision itself, evidence of how dependent markets became on Fed communication rather than the policy action.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Quantifies how much rates-market pricing is currently driven by Fed messaging rather than substance, a source of risk if that communication channel is scaled back.
insights.quote: 68% of the variance in the 5-year Treasury yield after a Fed meeting was attributable to the statement, and only 12% to the actual rate decision itself.

insights.claim: Forward guidance as a policy tool was architected by Ben Bernanke via two 2004 speeches ('Fed Speak' and 'Central Bank Talk and Monetary Policy'), designed to influence long-term rates at a time before the Fed had tools like QE to do so directly.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Understanding forward guidance's origin as a pre-QE workaround helps investors judge whether Warsh's rollback of it is a durable regime change or a cyclical stylistic shift.

insights.claim: A shift away from Fed forward guidance likely means higher market volatility and a larger bond term premium, and could specifically benefit trend-following strategies, since an incrementally revealed, data-dependent tightening cycle takes markets longer to fully price in than one pre-announced via a dot plot.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: A structural Fed communication shift could become a tailwind for trend-following returns going forward, and for term premium in bonds.

insights.claim: The AI capex boom is creating an inflation dilemma for the Fed: memory chips, electricity, and consumer products from iPhones to gaming consoles are being pushed higher by AI-driven demand now, while the Fed expects the productivity gains from AI to eventually be disinflationary, without knowing when that relief will arrive.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: An inflation overshoot risk exists near-term even if AI is disinflationary long-run, which could keep the Fed cautious about cutting rates.
insights.quote: We believe that we'll get a disinflationary impulse down the line, but we don't know when it is.

insights.claim: Despite markets pricing a hawkish read on Warsh after his first US press conference, the hosts believe consensus may be overestimating his hawkishness, since he separately said at a European conference that inflation risks are coming down.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: If the market's hawkish read on Warsh is wrong, rate expectations could adjust dovishly, a two-sided risk for rates positioning.

insights.claim: Apple raised its prices roughly 20-25% in a single day, an increase notable enough that the hosts expect it to show up in official inflation data.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable corporate pricing data point with a likely read-through into upcoming CPI prints.
insights.quote: Apple raising their prices 20-25% in a day

insights.claim: Beyond communication style, the more consequential change to watch under Warsh may be shifts to which economic data series the Fed collects and publishes and how they are calculated, since many market participants and models are built around the current data definitions.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Watch for methodology changes to key Fed-referenced data series, which could disrupt models and trading signals built on current definitions.

themes.theme: CTA and trend performance, first half of 2026

insights.claim: Niels' trend barometer stood at 50 as of early July 2026, a decent-to-strong reading, though performance had gone quiet-to-soft in the first week of July before reversing on renewed Middle East bombing campaigns, particularly in energy markets.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A live, weekly-updated gauge of how broadly CTAs are trending, useful for tracking near-term systematic-strategy positioning shifts.

insights.claim: As of July 7, 2026, systematic benchmarks were solidly positive year-to-date: the SG CTA Index +8.9%, SG Trend Index +8.5%, and Short-Term Traders Index +4.65%, versus MSCI World +9.65%, the US Aggregate Bond Index +0.25%, and S&P 500 total return near +10%.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete cross-asset performance scoreboard investors can use to benchmark CTA/trend allocations against equities and bonds for H1 2026.

insights.claim: First-half 2026 CTA performance showed notable dispersion: managers running non-trend strategies inside their books generally did somewhat better, while managers heavily exposed to previously popular 'alternative markets' mostly struggled, with few exceptions such as ISAM posting positive returns.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Manager selection within trend/CTA allocations should weight strategy composition (trend vs non-trend sleeves, alt-market exposure), not just headline category returns.

insights.claim: From an allocator's vantage point, June 2026 was a weak month for many CTA managers versus a benchmark down under 2%, with commodity reversals in aluminum, copper, and soybean oil tied to a change in Middle East tone, followed by a rebound in corn heading into July.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Highlights which specific commodity reversals drove June's below-benchmark manager dispersion, useful for post-mortem attribution.

insights.claim: The recent bullish 'commodity supercycle' narrative around energy and precious metals closely echoes the setup seen in March-April 2022 after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, a period that in hindsight turned out to be an excellent year for CTAs, though the hosts stop short of forecasting a repeat.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A narrative-driven commodity rally worth monitoring as a potential parallel to 2022's strong CTA year, without treating it as a forecast.

insights.claim: Fears in March 2026 that Middle East conflict would trigger a major Strait of Hormuz-driven oil price spike did not materialize, largely because China drew down its own reserves and rerouted oil flows via pipelines, letting the market absorb weeks of disruption.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Shows that 'this time it's different' crisis narratives around oil chokepoints can be overstated when China has spare reserve capacity to buffer supply shocks.

insights.claim: Copper is viewed as a key read on the AI infrastructure theme, with hyperscaler capex and data-center buildout plans continuing to escalate, a demand-side commodity driver that is structurally distinct from supply-shock narratives like Middle East oil disruptions.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Copper's AI-linked demand story is a durable secular trade thesis investors can track separately from geopolitical supply shocks.

themes.theme: Regime-change case for trend following (Aspect and Systematica papers)

insights.claim: Aspect Capital's paper argues the last 25 years of abundant, cheap energy and globalization's 'peace dividend' were a historical anomaly, and markets are now in a period of structural breaks, including bonds becoming less reliable equity diversifiers, the dollar's store-of-value status being reassessed, and central banks aggressively buying gold.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Supports strategic, not just tactical, allocations to trend following and commodities as portfolio diversifiers in a regime-change environment.

insights.claim: Investors shouldn't assume future macro shocks will only come from oil as in the 1970s template; shocks could just as easily emerge from copper, food prices, a fiscal crisis hitting bond markets, or a large dollar rally or decline, each requiring different diversifying exposures.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Broadens the tail-risk-hedging playbook beyond energy-shock-focused diversifiers to fiscal and currency shock scenarios.

insights.claim: Systematica's paper makes the case for trend following through a 'total portfolio approach' lens, arguing that with stock-bond correlation shifting and other hedge fund strategies (and private equity) carrying embedded equity risk, trend is a valuable complement to equity market-neutral strategies because it offers convexity that market-neutral lacks.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Allocators building genuine diversifier sleeves should pair trend with equity market-neutral rather than relying on bonds alone.

insights.claim: The broader industry conversation is shifting from 'which assets should I own' (e.g., the classic 60/40 stock-bond split) to 'which behavioral exposures or regimes should I own,' reflecting a total-portfolio mindset that evaluates a strategy by its marginal impact on the whole portfolio rather than its performance relative to similar-style peers.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A framework shift investors and allocators should adopt when evaluating whether to add trend following or other diversifying strategies.

themes.theme: Microstructure explanation for fast trend following's decline (CFM paper)

insights.claim: A new paper co-authored by Jean-Philippe Bouchaud (chairman of CFM) argues the primary driver of fast/short-term trend following's decline is volatility-adjusted tick size, dismissing two commonly cited explanations: industry AUM growth (inconsistent, since AUM stagnated for years while futures market liquidity grew) and market electronification (which shows no impact on interest-rate futures profitability).
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Reframes the trend-following 'AUM got too big' narrative as empirically unsupported and points allocators toward a different, testable driver of returns.

insights.claim: In small-tick markets like currencies (e.g., EUR/USD with a tick around 0.00001), advertised bid/offer liquidity understates true depth, whereas large-tick markets such as bonds show liquidity more transparently at the touch, a structural feature the CFM paper ties directly to fast trend following's diverging performance across asset classes.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Suggests fast-trend allocators should segment markets by tick-size regime rather than treating currencies and bonds as equally suited to short-term signals.

insights.claim: Before roughly 2010, large trend-following orders in FX (e.g., a $500 million to $1 billion dollar-yen trade) could be executed with big banks who would warehouse and pass on the risk to other banks, creating a cascading 'transmission mechanism' that itself generated further momentum in the market, an execution-driven effect the CFM paper argues was a major, underappreciated source of fast trend following's historical profitability.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Explains why fast trend outperformed pre-2010 independent of behavioral underreaction/overreaction theories, and why its edge eroded once that execution channel closed.

insights.claim: Since around 2010, banks have stepped back from warehousing large trade risk as high-frequency trading grew, breaking the FX and futures execution transmission mechanism that used to amplify trend-following orders into further momentum, a structural rather than cyclical change.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: If the transmission mechanism is structurally gone, fast trend's historical edge in small-tick markets may not return even as volatility cycles turn.

insights.claim: Per the CFM paper, fast trend following's underperformance since 2010 is concentrated specifically in small-tick markets like equities and currencies; in large-tick markets it has kept working, even outperforming, across the full sample back to 1995, with no evidence of a 'CTA winter' in that segment.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Allocators should differentiate fast-trend allocations by tick-size regime rather than treating short-term trend as uniformly broken.

insights.claim: The paper's Figure 10 shows fast trend following's underperformance was concentrated specifically between 2010 and 2020, with a partial recovery since 2020, leaving open whether this reflects a permanent structural break or simply a difficult decade, a question the paper itself does not resolve.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: An open empirical question worth independent testing before assuming fast trend is permanently impaired versus merely cyclically out of favor.

insights.claim: The CFM paper finds that market participants have shifted toward more counter-trend, mean-reverting trading behavior recently, corroborating a similar observation made separately by Toby Crabel (author of Opening Range Breakout) that breakouts now see less follow-through and more frequent reversals at short time horizons.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Independent corroboration from a veteran short-term trader strengthens the case that intraday/short-horizon market behavior has genuinely become more mean-reverting.

insights.claim: The paper finds trend following actually performs better in low-volatility environments, and that what has specifically been eliminated is trend followers' ability to profit from large one-day directional moves, a combination of liquidity and contract tick-size characteristics rather than liquidity alone, which did not explain the effect cross-sectionally.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Suggests trend-following risk models should weight tick-size and one-day-move capture ability, not just realized volatility, when sizing fast signals.

insights.claim: Testing his own firm's models, Alan Dunne confirmed independently that short-term-timeframe classical trend-following signals have been unprofitable for roughly the past 15 years, with a break point around 2009 that matches the CFM paper's findings.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Independent, real-world confirmation of the paper's core empirical claim from a practitioner who tested it against live systematic models.

insights.claim: Niels offers three alternative or complementary explanations for fast trend's decline beyond tick size: 2009-era banking reforms that restricted proprietary trading and pushed liquidity provision to be outsourced; the rise of multi-strategy 'pod shop' AUM, whose managers can reportedly be forced to cut risk by 50% within two hours of a Friday-afternoon risk call; and the fact that when exchanges shrink contract size (like CME's new 10-barrel oil contract), traders must buy far more contracts for the same notional exposure while per-contract fees likely stay flat, raising effective commission costs.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Multiple candidate drivers besides tick size should be independently tested before attributing fast trend's decline to a single cause; smaller futures contract sizes may quietly raise trading costs for CTAs.
insights.quote: If you're one of the pods and you get a call on a Friday afternoon that you need to cut your risk by 50%, they give you two hours to cut that risk.

insights.claim: A shift in execution style, from manually working paper order tickets during the day to algorithmic execution that goes straight from signal to exchange, is another likely factor behind changed short-term market dynamics, since execution algorithms behave differently than human traders by having no emotion and more patience.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Execution-technology evolution is an underappreciated, independently testable contributor to changed short-term market microstructure.

themes.theme: Retail access to professional derivatives markets

insights.claim: CME Group is launching a new oil futures contract sized at 10 barrels versus the standard 1,000-barrel contract, sharply cutting the contract size to draw more retail money into the market, a move Niels questions given that some financial instruments may not be well-suited for widespread retail trading.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Signals continued exchange-level efforts to retailize professional derivatives markets, worth watching for participation and flow effects.

insights.claim: Niels cites last year's silver retail buying frenzy, where in his view it wasn't professionals who bought the highs, as a cautionary example of retail money entering markets late and near tops when access is broadened.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A live case study of retail-driven late-cycle buying risk relevant to any newly retail-accessible market, such as CME's smaller oil contract.

insights.claim: Alan Dunne counters that retail participation in commodities is largely unavoidable regardless of new contract design, since retail traders already access oil exposure via ETFs (which broke down when oil prices went negative) and spread betting, so CME offering smaller contracts is simply formalizing an existing retail segment.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Suggests new small-size contracts mainly channel existing retail demand into exchange-traded products rather than creating new speculative risk from scratch.

themes.theme: Jeremy Grantham's early quant investing history

insights.claim: A new biography of Jeremy Grantham, co-written with Edward Chancellor, reveals that Grantham's early firm (predating GMO) was an early pioneer of both passive investing and quantitative investing, and saw strong complementarity between value and momentum factors, a contrast to Grantham's public image as a purely fundamental, bottom-up value bear.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: Reframes a famous 'perma-bear' fundamental investor as an early systematic and quant pioneer, relevant context for how long value-momentum complementarity has been recognized.

insights.claim: Grantham's team built an early quant equity factor model for a client, the IMF, that initially underperformed and was shelved, was later improved and worked well, but after roughly 10 years of further attempted enhancements the team couldn't meaningfully improve the model any further.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: A cautionary data point for allocators evaluating managers who claim continuous alpha improvements to legacy quant models.

insights.claim: Grantham's team also identified a 'neglect factor,' stocks with low analyst coverage such as small caps, that tested well in combination with value and momentum but stopped working for roughly six years immediately after being implemented, a decades-old illustration that a well-researched factor can go dark for years once deployed.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunne
insights.so_what: A historical parallel to today's fast-trend-following drought, reinforcing that long dry spells in systematic factors are not new and shouldn't automatically be read as a strategy being permanently broken.
